seoTitle: PineSemi Products | SiC MOSFETs, IGBT Modules, Power Semiconductors
seoDescription: Browse PineSemi's comprehensive power semiconductor portfolio including SiC MOSFETs, IGBT modules, intelligent power modules, and SiC diodes for EV, renewable energy, and industrial applications.

faqs.question: How do I select the right PineSemi SiC MOSFET for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right SiC MOSFET involves several key parameters: 1) Voltage rating - choose Vds rating at least 20% higher than maximum system voltage; 2) Current rating - select based on RMS current with thermal considerations; 3) On-resistance (Rds(on)) - lower is better for conduction losses; 4) Package - consider thermal performance and PCB layout; 5) Gate charge - affects switching losses and driver requirements. For EV traction inverters, 1200V devices are typical. For onboard chargers and DC-DC, 650V devices are common. Contact our FAE team for detailed selection guidance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our SiC MOSFET selection guide or contact FAE for application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PineSemi SiC MOSFETs and IGBTs?
faqs.answer: SiC MOSFETs and IGBTs have different characteristics: SiC MOSFETs offer lower switching losses, higher frequency operation, better high-temperature performance, and no tail current. They're ideal for high-frequency applications above 20kHz. IGBTs have lower conduction losses at high current, lower cost per amp, and mature ecosystem. They're better for lower frequency, high-current applications. PineSemi offers both technologies - choose SiC for EV, solar, and high-frequency applications; choose IGBT for motor drives, welders, and cost-sensitive applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed comparison and application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: What packages are available for PineSemi power devices?
faqs.answer: PineSemi offers various package options: Discrete packages - TO-247, TO-263 (D2PAK), TO-252 (DPAK) for through-hole and surface mount; Advanced packages - TO-247-4 with Kelvin source for improved switching; Modules - standard industrial modules in various configurations; Custom modules - application-specific designs available for high volume. Package selection depends on power level, thermal requirements, and manufacturing process. Higher power applications typically use modules for better thermal performance and lower parasitic inductance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for package recommendations based on your power and thermal requirements.

categories.name: SiC MOSFETs
categories.description: PineSemi's silicon carbide MOSFETs deliver superior switching performance, low on-resistance, and high-temperature operation for next-generation power electronics.
categories.longDescription: PineSemi's silicon carbide (SiC) MOSFET portfolio represents the cutting edge of power semiconductor technology. These devices leverage the unique properties of wide bandgap SiC material to deliver breakthrough performance compared to traditional silicon devices. With breakdown voltages from 650V to 1200V and on-resistance as low as 15mΩ, PineSemi SiC MOSFETs enable unprecedented efficiency and power density in power conversion applications. The superior thermal conductivity of SiC allows operation at higher junction temperatures (up to 175°C), simplifying thermal management and enabling more compact designs. Key features include ultra-fast switching with minimal switching losses, zero reverse recovery charge in the body diode, and excellent avalanche capability. These characteristics make PineSemi SiC MOSFETs the ideal choice for demanding applications such as EV traction inverters, onboard chargers, DC-DC converters, solar inverters, and high-frequency power supplies. The devices are available in industry-standard packages including TO-247, TO-263, and advanced Kelvin-source packages for optimal switching performance.

series.name: PSM1 Series
series.description: 650V SiC MOSFETs for onboard chargers and DC-DC converters

series.name: PSM2 Series
series.description: 1200V SiC MOSFETs for EV traction inverters and industrial drives

selectionGuide.title: How to Select PineSemi SiC MOSFETs
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting SiC MOSFETs based on voltage, current, and application requirements

faqs.question: What are the key advantages of PineSemi SiC MOSFETs over silicon IGBTs?
faqs.answer: PineSemi SiC MOSFETs offer significant advantages: 1) Switching losses reduced by 50-80% enabling higher frequency operation; 2) No tail current eliminates turn-off losses; 3) Lower on-resistance temperature coefficient provides better high-temperature performance; 4) Higher switching frequencies (up to 100kHz+) allow smaller magnetics; 5) Higher efficiency across load range; 6) Better thermal performance with higher junction temperature capability. These advantages translate to system-level benefits including smaller size, lower weight, reduced cooling requirements, and higher overall efficiency. For EV applications, this means extended driving range and faster charging.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical analysis comparing SiC and IGBT for your specific application.

faqs.question: How do I drive PineSemi SiC MOSFETs properly?
faqs.answer: Proper gate drive is critical for SiC MOSFET performance: Gate voltage - use +15V to +20V for turn-on, -3V to -5V for turn-off; Gate resistance - typically 5-20Ω depending on switching speed and EMI requirements; Driver current - ensure adequate source/sink current for fast switching; Isolation - use isolated gate drivers for high-voltage applications; Protection - implement desaturation detection and soft shutdown. PineSemi recommends specific gate driver ICs and provides reference designs. The Kelvin-source connection (available in TO-247-4 package) reduces switching losses by eliminating source inductance effects. Always follow PineSemi's application notes for gate drive design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for gate driver recommendations and reference designs for PineSemi SiC MOSFETs.

faqs.question: What thermal management is required for PineSemi SiC MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Thermal design considerations for SiC MOSFETs: Junction temperature - can operate up to 175°C (Tj max); Heat sinking - calculate based on Rds(on) at operating temperature and switching losses; Thermal interface - use high-quality thermal grease or pads; PCB design - use thick copper and thermal vias for SMD packages; Operating temperature - account for Rds(on) increase with temperature (lower temp coefficient than silicon). While SiC devices run cooler than silicon for the same losses, proper thermal design is still essential. PineSemi provides thermal models and application notes for thermal design. Simulation tools can help optimize heat sink selection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for thermal design support and simulation assistance.

faqs.question: Can PineSemi SiC MOSFETs be paralleled for higher current?
faqs.answer: Yes, PineSemi SiC MOSFETs can be paralleled for higher current capability: Requirements - use devices from same production lot for matched characteristics; Layout - ensure symmetric PCB layout with equal trace lengths and impedances; Gate drive - use individual gate resistors and consider separate gate drivers; Current sharing - positive temperature coefficient of Rds(on) promotes natural current sharing; Protection - implement individual overcurrent protection. Paralleling 2-4 devices is common practice. For higher power, consider using larger single devices or modules instead of many parallel discrete devices. PineSemi offers modules with parallel dies already matched and integrated.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for paralleling guidelines and module alternatives for high-current applications.

faqs.question: What is the typical gate threshold voltage for PineSemi SiC MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: PineSemi SiC MOSFETs typically have gate threshold voltages (Vgs(th)) in the range of 2.5V to 3.5V at 25°C. However, Vgs(th) has a negative temperature coefficient of approximately -10mV/°C, meaning threshold voltage decreases as temperature increases. This characteristic requires careful gate drive design to prevent false turn-on from noise or dv/dt coupling. Recommended gate drive voltages: Turn-on: +15V to +20V (ensures low Rds(on)); Turn-off: -3V to -5V (prevents false turn-on); Miller plateau voltage: typically 5-8V during switching. Always use negative gate voltage for off-state to ensure noise immunity, especially at high temperatures. PineSemi provides detailed gate charge characteristics in datasheets.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for gate drive design recommendations and application notes.

products.name: 650V 35mΩ SiC MOSFET
products.shortDescription: 650V silicon carbide MOSFET with 35mΩ on-resistance in TO-247 package, ideal for onboard chargers and DC-DC converters.
descriptionParagraphs: The PSM1-65R035 is a 650V SiC MOSFET featuring 35mΩ typical on-resistance at Vgs=18V.
This device delivers exceptional switching performance with total switching energy below 200μJ at 400V/20A.
Housed in industry-standard TO-247 package, it offers drop-in replacement capability for existing designs.
features: Low on-resistance for reduced conduction losses
Fast switching with minimal switching losses
Zero reverse recovery charge in body diode

faeReview.author: Dr. Zhang Wei
faeReview.title: Principal FAE - Power Electronics
faeReview.content: The PSM1-65R035 has become my go-to recommendation for 650V SiC applications. I've evaluated this device extensively in EV onboard charger designs and the performance is impressive. The 35mΩ Rds(on) provides excellent efficiency in hard-switching applications, and the switching characteristics are well-balanced for EMI performance. In a recent 11kW OBC design, replacing silicon superjunction MOSFETs with these SiC devices improved full-load efficiency by 1.8% and reduced heatsink volume by 40%. The AEC-Q101 qualification gives automotive customers confidence for production designs. The TO-247 package makes it easy to evaluate and prototype without custom PCBs.
faeReview.highlight: Excellent performance for onboard chargers with proven automotive qualification

alternativeParts.reason: Lower Rds(on) for higher current applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for higher power onboard chargers or when lower conduction losses are needed

alternativeParts.reason: Lower cost option for lower current applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for lower power applications where cost is critical

companionParts.description: 650V 60A SiC Schottky diode for PFC applications
Isolated gate driver for SiC MOSFETs
Thermal interface pad for TO-247 package

faqs.question: What is the recommended gate drive voltage for PSM1-65R035?
faqs.answer: Recommended gate drive voltages for PSM1-65R035: Turn-on voltage: +18V (ensures lowest Rds(on)); Turn-off voltage: -3V to -5V (prevents false turn-on); Gate resistance: 10Ω typical (5-20Ω range depending on switching speed needs). The device is fully enhanced at +15V, but +18V provides margin and lowest on-resistance. Negative gate voltage is strongly recommended for noise immunity, especially at high dv/dt conditions. Gate driver should provide at least 2A source/sink current for fast switching. Always use a gate resistor to limit switching speed and control EMI. For high-frequency applications, consider using Kelvin-source connection (TO-247-4 version) to eliminate source inductance effects.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for gate driver recommendations and reference designs.

faqs.question: How does Rds(on) vary with temperature for PSM1-65R035?
faqs.answer: The PSM1-65R035 exhibits positive temperature coefficient for Rds(on): At 25°C: 35mΩ typical; At 100°C: approximately 50mΩ; At 150°C: approximately 65mΩ. This represents about 85% increase from 25°C to 150°C. While this seems significant, it's much better than silicon superjunction MOSFETs which can increase by 150% or more over the same temperature range. For thermal design, use Rds(on) at maximum expected junction temperature to calculate conduction losses. The positive temperature coefficient is beneficial for paralleling devices as it promotes current sharing - devices running hotter will have higher resistance and carry less current.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for thermal design support and loss calculation tools.

faqs.question: What is the body diode performance of PSM1-65R035?
faqs.answer: The PSM1-65R035 includes an intrinsic body diode with these characteristics: Forward voltage: approximately 3.5V at rated current (higher than silicon); Reverse recovery charge (Qrr): essentially zero - this is a key advantage of SiC; Reverse recovery time (trr): very fast, typically <50ns; Continuous diode current: same as MOSFET current rating. The zero reverse recovery makes the body diode suitable for hard-switching applications without external anti-parallel diodes. However, the high forward voltage drop means significant conduction losses when using the body diode. For applications with significant diode conduction time (like bridge configurations), external SiC Schottky diodes are recommended for better efficiency.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for diode selection and synchronous rectification design guidance.

faqs.question: Can PSM1-65R035 be used in bridge configurations?
faqs.answer: Yes, PSM1-65R035 can be used in bridge configurations such as full-bridge inverters and totem-pole PFC: Advantages - zero reverse recovery eliminates switching losses in hard-switched bridges; fast switching enables high-frequency operation; low switching losses reduce heatsink requirements. Considerations - use appropriate dead time to prevent shoot-through; consider external SiC Schottky diodes if diode conduction losses are significant; implement proper gate drive with negative voltage for noise immunity; pay attention to layout to minimize parasitic inductance. For totem-pole PFC, the fast switching and zero Qrr make SiC ideal for the high-frequency leg. For motor drive inverters, SiC enables higher switching frequencies for improved motor performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for bridge configuration design guides and layout recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the short-circuit withstand capability of PSM1-65R035?
faqs.answer: The PSM1-65R035 has short-circuit withstand time of approximately 2-3μs at Vds=400V and Vgs=18V. This is shorter than typical IGBTs (10μs) due to the higher current density and smaller die size of SiC devices. For short-circuit protection: Detection - use desaturation detection circuits with typical 1-2μs detection time; Response - gate driver must shut down within 1μs of detection; Soft shutdown - implement soft turn-off to prevent voltage overshoot. The short-circuit capability is sufficient for most applications when proper protection is implemented. Always design protection circuits with margin - target total response time < 2μs. PineSemi provides application notes on short-circuit protection design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for short-circuit protection design guidelines and recommended gate drivers.

products.name: 1200V 40mΩ SiC MOSFET
products.shortDescription: 1200V silicon carbide MOSFET with 40mΩ on-resistance, designed for EV traction inverters and high-voltage industrial applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The PSM2-120R040 is a 1200V SiC MOSFET featuring 40mΩ typical on-resistance for high-voltage power conversion applications.
Designed specifically for demanding applications such as EV traction inverters, this device delivers exceptional efficiency and reliability.
Available in both standard TO-247 and Kelvin-source TO-247-4 packages for optimized switching performance.

faeReview.author: Li Ming
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Automotive Power
faeReview.content: The PSM2-120R040 is specifically designed for 800V EV traction inverters, and it delivers outstanding performance in this demanding application. I've worked with several EV manufacturers evaluating this device, and the results consistently exceed expectations. In a 150kW traction inverter design, these devices achieved 98.5% peak efficiency at 20kHz switching frequency - something impossible with silicon IGBTs. The Kelvin-source package is a game-changer for high-current applications, reducing switching losses by approximately 15% compared to standard 3-pin packages. The AEC-Q101 qualification with full automotive-grade reliability testing gives OEMs confidence for production programs. For any 800V EV powertrain application, this is my top recommendation.
faeReview.highlight: Purpose-built for 800V EV traction inverters with exceptional efficiency

alternativeParts.reason: Lower Rds(on) for higher power traction inverters
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for high-power EV traction inverters (>150kW)

alternativeParts.reason: Lower cost option for lower current applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for auxiliary inverters or lower power applications

companionParts.description: High-isolation gate driver for automotive applications

faqs.question: What makes the Kelvin-source package better for high-current applications?
faqs.answer: The Kelvin-source (TO-247-4) package provides a separate source connection for the gate drive circuit: Standard TO-247 - gate drive current flows through main source pin, creating voltage drop across source inductance; Kelvin-source - gate drive has separate pin, eliminating source inductance from gate loop. Benefits: 1) Faster switching - no source inductance feedback slows turn-on; 2) Lower switching losses - typically 10-20% reduction; 3) Better control - gate voltage more accurately controlled; 4) Higher frequency capability - reduced switching times allow higher frequencies. For high-current applications (>30A) or high-frequency switching (>50kHz), the Kelvin-source package is strongly recommended. The improvement is most noticeable in hard-switching applications with high di/dt.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Kelvin-source package for high-current or high-frequency applications. Contact us for layout guidelines.

faqs.question: How does the PSM2-120R040 perform in automotive qualification testing?
faqs.answer: The PSM2-120R040 has passed full AEC-Q101 qualification testing including: High temperature reverse bias (HTRB) - 1000 hours at 100% rated voltage and 175°C; High temperature gate bias (HTGB) - 1000 hours at maximum Vgs and 175°C; Temperature cycling (TC) - 1000 cycles -55°C to +175°C; High humidity high temperature reverse bias (H3TRB) - 1000 hours at 85°C/85% RH; Intermittent operating life (IOL) - 50,000 cycles at rated current; Electrostatic discharge (ESD) - HBM and CDM testing. All tests passed with zero failures. The device meets Grade 0 requirements for under-hood automotive applications. Full qualification reports and PPAP documentation are available for automotive customers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for qualification reports and automotive PPAP documentation.

faqs.question: What is the recommended layout for PSM2-120R040 in traction inverter designs?
faqs.answer: PCB layout recommendations for PSM2-120R040 in traction inverters: Minimize parasitic inductance - keep power loops as small as possible; Use laminated busbars for DC link connections to minimize inductance; Gate drive placement - place gate driver close to MOSFET, minimize gate loop area; Kelvin connection - use separate traces for Kelvin source connection, don't connect to power source; Decoupling - place ceramic capacitors close to device for high-frequency decoupling; Thermal vias - use thermal vias under drain pad for SMD versions; Clearance - maintain adequate creepage and clearance for 800V operation. Good layout is critical for achieving datasheet performance and reliable operation. PineSemi provides reference designs and layout guidelines for traction inverter applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for reference designs and layout review services for your traction inverter design.

faqs.question: Can PSM2-120R040 be used in parallel for high-power traction inverters?
faqs.answer: Yes, PSM2-120R040 devices can be paralleled for high-power traction inverters: Typical configuration - 2-4 devices per switch position for 150-300kW inverters; Current sharing - positive temperature coefficient of Rds(on) promotes natural current sharing; Symmetry requirements - matched trace lengths and impedances for all parallel devices; Gate drive - individual gate resistors recommended, consider separate drivers for each device; Protection - implement individual desaturation detection for each device. For a 150kW traction inverter, typical configuration uses 2 devices in parallel per switch position (12 devices total for 3-phase bridge). For higher power, consider using lower Rds(on) devices or modules instead of many parallel discrete devices. PineSemi also offers modules with parallel dies already integrated.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for paralleling guidelines and module alternatives for high-power traction inverters.

faqs.question: What are the EMI considerations when using PSM2-120R040?
faqs.answer: EMI considerations for PSM2-120R040 high-speed switching: dv/dt - SiC devices have very fast dv/dt (>50V/ns) which can cause conducted and radiated EMI; di/dt - fast current transitions can induce voltages in parasitic inductances; Gate drive - proper gate resistance selection balances switching speed vs EMI; Layout - minimize loop areas, use proper shielding and filtering; Common mode - fast switching generates common-mode noise requiring filtering. Mitigation strategies: Use appropriate gate resistance (typically 10-20Ω) to control switching speed; Implement proper input and output filters; Use shielded gate drive transformers or optocouplers; Follow good PCB layout practices; Consider using soft-switching topologies where possible. While SiC enables high efficiency, the fast switching requires careful EMI design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for EMI design guidelines and filter design recommendations.

categories.name: IGBT Modules
categories.description: PineSemi's IGBT modules deliver high-efficiency power switching for industrial drives, renewable energy, and power supply applications.
categories.longDescription: PineSemi's IGBT module portfolio combines advanced trench-gate field-stop technology with robust packaging to deliver superior performance in high-power applications. These modules feature low conduction losses, soft switching characteristics, and excellent short-circuit withstand capability. Available in voltage ratings from 600V to 1700V and current ratings from 50A to 600A, PineSemi IGBT modules serve diverse applications including industrial motor drives, solar and wind inverters, UPS systems, and welding equipment. The modules incorporate advanced features such as integrated NTC temperature sensors, low-inductance design, and direct-cooled baseplates for high-power density. Standard industrial packages ensure compatibility with existing inverter designs while enabling higher efficiency and reliability.

series.name: PIM Series
series.description: Six-pack IGBT modules for motor drive applications

series.name: PFC Series
series.description: IGBT modules optimized for power factor correction

selectionGuide.title: How to Select PineSemi IGBT Modules
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting IGBT modules based on voltage, current, and application requirements

faqs.question: When should I choose IGBT over SiC MOSFET?
faqs.answer: Choose IGBT over SiC MOSFET when: 1) Switching frequency is below 20kHz - IGBTs have lower conduction losses at low frequencies; 2) Cost is critical - IGBTs are 2-3x lower cost per amp; 3) Short-circuit withstand is important - IGBTs typically have 10μs vs 2-3μs for SiC; 4) Mature ecosystem is needed - IGBT gate drivers and application support are more established; 5) High current capability is required - IGBT modules easily achieve 600A+ per switch. Applications where IGBT is preferred: Industrial motor drives (<10kHz), welders, induction heating, grid-tied inverters (<10kHz). For high-frequency or high-efficiency applications, SiC is usually better despite higher cost.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed comparison and application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between trench-gate and planar IGBTs?
faqs.answer: Trench-gate IGBTs offer advantages over planar technology: Structure - trench-gate has vertical channel vs lateral in planar; Conduction losses - trench-gate typically 15-25% lower Vce(sat); Switching losses - trench-gate has controlled switching with lower tail current; Cell density - trench allows higher cell density for better current capability; Ruggedness - modern trench designs have excellent SOA and short-circuit capability. PineSemi uses advanced trench-gate field-stop technology combining the best characteristics of both NPT and PT IGBTs. This provides low conduction losses, fast switching, and excellent ruggedness. All PineSemi IGBT modules use trench-gate technology for optimal performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose trench-gate IGBTs for new designs. Contact us for specific device recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I calculate losses in IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: IGBT module loss calculation: Conduction losses - Pcond = Vce(sat) x Ic x duty cycle, use Vce(sat) at operating temperature; Switching losses - Psw = (Eon + Eoff) x switching frequency; Diode losses - Pdiode = Vf x If x duty cycle + Err x frequency; Total losses - sum of IGBT conduction, IGBT switching, and diode losses. Use datasheet values at operating conditions (temperature, current). For accurate calculations, consider: Temperature effects - Vce(sat) increases with temperature; Current dependency - losses are non-linear with current; Switching frequency - higher frequency increases switching losses. PineSemi provides simulation models and application notes for loss calculation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for loss calculation tools and thermal design support.

faqs.question: What gate drive voltage is recommended for PineSemi IGBTs?
faqs.answer: Recommended gate drive for PineSemi IGBTs: Turn-on voltage: +15V (standard), ensures low Vce(sat); Turn-off voltage: -8V to -15V (negative voltage recommended), improves noise immunity and reduces turn-off losses; Gate resistance: 5-50Ω depending on switching speed requirements; Drive current: adequate to charge/discharge gate capacitance quickly. Negative gate voltage is strongly recommended for: High power applications, noisy environments, parallel operation, and high dv/dt conditions. The gate driver should provide at least 2x the required average current for fast switching. Always use a series gate resistor to limit di/dt and control EMI. Follow PineSemi's application notes for specific recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for gate driver recommendations and reference designs.

faqs.question: How do I protect IGBT modules from overcurrent?
faqs.answer: IGBT overcurrent protection methods: Desaturation detection - monitors Vce during conduction, triggers at 6-8V typical; Current sensing - use shunt resistors or Rogowski coils for current monitoring; Soft shutdown - gradual gate voltage reduction prevents voltage overshoot; Gate clamping - prevents gate overvoltage during switching; Snubber circuits - limit voltage overshoot during turn-off. Protection timing is critical: Detection time: < 2μs; Shutdown time: < 1μs after detection; Total fault clearance: < 10μs (within IGBT short-circuit capability). Modern gate drivers integrate desaturation detection and soft shutdown. Always design protection with margin - don't operate near limits. PineSemi provides application notes on protection circuit design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for protection design guidelines and recommended gate drivers with integrated protection.

products.name: 75A 600V Six-Pack IGBT Module
products.shortDescription: 75A 600V six-pack IGBT module for motor drives and inverters, featuring trench-gate technology and integrated temperature sensor.
descriptionParagraphs: The PIM-75A-6 is a 75A 600V six-pack IGBT module designed for 3-phase motor drive applications.
Featuring advanced trench-gate field-stop technology for low conduction and switching losses.
Integrated NTC temperature sensor and low-inductance design enable compact and reliable inverter designs.
features: Low Vce(sat) for reduced conduction losses

faeReview.author: Wang Hao
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Industrial Drives
faeReview.content: The PIM-75A-6 is an excellent choice for general-purpose motor drives up to 30kW. I've used this module in numerous industrial drive designs and it consistently delivers reliable performance. The 1.65V Vce(sat) is competitive with international brands, and the switching characteristics are well-balanced for motor drive applications where EMI is a concern. The integrated temperature sensor simplifies thermal management design - no need for external sensors. In a recent 15kW servo drive project, this module achieved 97.2% efficiency at full load. The standard 34mm package makes it compatible with many existing inverter designs. For cost-sensitive industrial applications, this module offers excellent value.
faeReview.highlight: Excellent value for industrial motor drives with proven reliability

alternativeParts.useCase: Use for drives up to 15kW or lower current applications

alternativeParts.reason: Higher current option for larger drives
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for drives up to 45kW requiring higher current

companionParts.description: IGBT gate driver with desaturation protection

faqs.question: What is the recommended switching frequency for PIM-75A-6?
faqs.answer: Recommended switching frequencies for PIM-75A-6: Motor drive applications: 4-16kHz typical, 20kHz maximum; Higher frequencies reduce motor noise and current ripple but increase switching losses; Lower frequencies improve efficiency but increase motor noise. For general-purpose drives: 6-8kHz is typical; For servo drives: 10-16kHz for better dynamic response; For HVAC applications: 4-6kHz for maximum efficiency. At 8kHz switching frequency with 400V DC bus and 75A output, total losses are approximately 180W per module. Thermal design should account for losses at the selected switching frequency. PineSemi provides loss curves for different operating conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for switching frequency optimization based on your application requirements.

faqs.question: How do I mount PIM-75A-6 to the heatsink?
faqs.answer: Mounting procedure for PIM-75A-6: Thermal interface - apply thermal grease or use thermal pad (0.1-0.2mm thick, 2-3 W/mK thermal conductivity); Mounting torque - 2.5-3.5 Nm for M4 screws (follow datasheet specification); Screw pattern - use cross-pattern tightening to ensure even pressure; Flatness - ensure heatsink surface flatness within 0.05mm; Surface finish - Ra < 3.2μm recommended. Important considerations: Don't overtighten - can damage module baseplate or die; Don't undertighten - poor thermal contact increases temperature; Use spring washers to maintain pressure over temperature cycling; Allow thermal interface material to spread properly. Proper mounting is critical for thermal performance. Poor mounting can increase thermal resistance by 50% or more.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for mounting guidelines and thermal interface material recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the thermal resistance of PIM-75A-6?
faqs.answer: Thermal resistance specifications for PIM-75A-6: Rth(j-c) IGBT - 0.45 K/W (junction to case); Rth(j-c) Diode - 0.65 K/W; Rth(c-s) - 0.05 K/W (case to heatsink) with thermal grease; Total Rth(j-s) - approximately 0.5 K/W. Temperature calculations: Tj = Ta + (Ptotal x Rth(j-a)); Where Rth(j-a) = Rth(j-c) + Rth(c-s) + Rth(s-a). Example: 150W losses, 0.5 K/W Rth(j-s), 50°C heatsink: Tj = 50 + (150 x 0.5) = 125°C. Maximum junction temperature is 150°C, so this provides 25°C margin. For reliable operation, keep Tj below 125°C under worst-case conditions. PineSemi provides thermal models and simulation files for detailed analysis.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for thermal design support and heatsink selection assistance.

faqs.question: Can PIM-75A-6 be used for braking chopper applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, PIM-75A-6 can be used for braking chopper applications: Configuration - use one IGBT/diode pair as braking switch; Current rating - 75A continuous, 150A peak for short duration; Voltage rating - 600V suitable for 400V DC bus systems; Switching frequency - typically 2-4kHz for braking choppers; Duty cycle - can operate at high duty cycles during braking. Design considerations: Ensure adequate heatsinking for braking power; Implement overcurrent protection; Consider using external brake resistor with proper rating; Monitor temperature during heavy braking cycles. For high-power braking, consider dedicated braking modules. The integrated temperature sensor helps monitor operating conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for braking chopper design guidelines and component selection.

faqs.question: What is the expected lifetime of PIM-75A-6?
faqs.answer: Expected lifetime for PIM-75A-6 depends on operating conditions: Power cycling - number of temperature cycles affects bond wire life; Thermal cycling - expansion mismatch between materials causes stress; Operating temperature - higher Tj reduces lifetime (Arrhenius relationship); Voltage stress - higher voltage margins improve reliability. Typical lifetime: 20 years at moderate operating conditions (Tj<125°C, limited power cycling); 10-15 years at demanding conditions (Tj<140°C, frequent cycling). Accelerated testing: Temperature cycling: > 50,000 cycles -55°C to +150°C; Power cycling: > 30,000 cycles at rated current; HTRB: > 1000 hours at rated voltage and 150°C. PineSemi modules meet industrial reliability standards. Contact us for lifetime prediction based on your specific operating profile.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for reliability data and lifetime prediction modeling for your application.

products.name: 50A 1200V IGBT Module for PFC
products.shortDescription: 50A 1200V IGBT module optimized for power factor correction applications with fast switching and low switching losses.
descriptionParagraphs: The PFC-50A-12 is a 50A 1200V IGBT module specifically designed for power factor correction and boost converter applications.
Optimized for hard-switching with fast switching characteristics and low switching losses.
Features low Qrr freewheeling diode for reduced diode recovery losses in bridgeless PFC applications.

faeReview.author: Chen Jun
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Power Conversion
faeReview.content: The PFC-50A-12 is specifically optimized for PFC applications, and it shows in the performance. I've used this module in several high-power PFC designs (10-20kW range) and the efficiency numbers are excellent. The fast switching characteristics enable operation at 30-40kHz with reasonable losses, allowing smaller magnetic components. The low Qrr diode is critical for bridgeless totem-pole PFC designs where diode recovery losses can dominate. In a recent 15kW server power supply design, this module achieved 99.1% PFC efficiency at full load and 50kHz switching. The soft switching characteristics also help with EMI - easier to meet CISPR standards. For high-power PFC applications, this module offers an excellent balance of performance and cost.
faeReview.highlight: Purpose-built for PFC with excellent efficiency at high switching frequencies

alternativeParts.reason: Lower current option for smaller PFC stages
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for PFC stages up to 10kW

alternativeParts.reason: SiC alternative for higher frequency operation
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for high-frequency PFC (>50kHz) where efficiency is critical

companionParts.description: Gate driver optimized for PFC applications

faqs.question: What switching frequency is optimal for PFC-50A-12?
faqs.answer: Optimal switching frequencies for PFC-50A-12: Standard PFC: 20-30kHz for good balance of efficiency and magnetic size; High-density designs: 40-60kHz for smaller magnetics; Bridgeless totem-pole: 50-100kHz (SiC recommended for >50kHz). At 30kHz with 400V output and 50A input, total losses are approximately 120W. Trade-offs: Lower frequency (20kHz) - higher efficiency, larger inductor, audible noise possible; Higher frequency (60kHz) - smaller inductor, lower efficiency, better power density. For server power supplies targeting 80Plus Titanium, 40-50kHz is typical. The module's fast switching enables these higher frequencies while maintaining good efficiency.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for PFC design optimization and magnetic component selection.

faqs.question: How does PFC-50A-12 compare to SiC MOSFETs for PFC?
faqs.answer: PFC-50A-12 vs SiC comparison for PFC: Efficiency - SiC offers 0.3-0.5% higher efficiency at same frequency; Frequency - SiC can switch at 2-3x higher frequency with reasonable losses; Cost - IGBT is 2-3x lower cost per amp; Complexity - IGBT gate drive is simpler and more mature; Inductor size - SiC enables 30-50% smaller inductor at high frequency. Decision factors: For cost-sensitive applications (<15kW): IGBT is usually better choice; For high-density designs: SiC may justify higher cost; For >50kHz operation: SiC is preferred; For 99%+ efficiency targets: SiC is necessary. Many designs use IGBT for standard PFC and SiC for bridgeless totem-pole PFC where diode recovery is critical.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for detailed comparison and PFC topology recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the diode recovery performance of PFC-50A-12?
faqs.answer: Diode characteristics of PFC-50A-12: Qrr (reverse recovery charge): 3.5μC typical at 125°C; trr (reverse recovery time): 120ns typical; Irrm (peak reverse current): 12A typical; Softness factor: >0.8 for soft recovery. These characteristics are optimized for PFC applications: Low Qrr reduces switching losses in hard-switched bridge configurations; Soft recovery reduces EMI and voltage overshoot; Fast recovery enables higher switching frequencies. For standard boost PFC, these diodes perform well. For bridgeless totem-pole PFC where diodes conduct continuously during half-line cycle, SiC Schottky diodes are recommended for best efficiency. The integrated diode is sufficient for most standard PFC applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for diode selection in bridgeless PFC applications.

faqs.question: Can PFC-50A-12 be used in interleaved PFC designs?
faqs.answer: Yes, PFC-50A-12 is well-suited for interleaved PFC designs: Benefits of interleaving: Reduced input/output ripple current; Smaller magnetic components; Better thermal distribution; Higher effective switching frequency. Configuration options: 2-phase interleaved - use 2 modules, 180° phase shift; 3-phase interleaved - use 3 modules, 120° phase shift. Current per module is total current divided by number of phases. For a 15kW PFC with 2-phase interleaving: Total current ~38A RMS, 19A per phase, well within 50A rating. Interleaving control requires current balancing between phases. PineSemi modules work well with standard interleaved PFC controllers. Thermal design is easier as losses are distributed across multiple modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for interleaved PFC design guidelines and control strategies.

faqs.question: What protection features are needed for PFC applications?
faqs.answer: Essential protection for PFC using PFC-50A-12: Overcurrent protection - desaturation detection or current sensing, < 10μs response; Overvoltage protection - output overvoltage detection to prevent capacitor damage; Overtemperature protection - use integrated NTC sensor, shutdown at 125°C heatsink; Inrush current limiting - soft-start or NTC thermistor for startup; Input undervoltage/overvoltage - protect from abnormal line conditions. Additional considerations: Reverse recovery protection - ensure adequate dead time to prevent shoot-through; Surge protection - MOV or TVS for lightning surge protection; Fuse protection - fast-acting fuses for catastrophic failure protection. PFC controllers typically integrate many protection features. External protection circuits should be designed with appropriate margins.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for PFC protection design guidelines and recommended components.

categories.name: Intelligent Power Modules
categories.description: PineSemi's intelligent power modules integrate power devices, gate drivers, and protection functions for simplified system design.
categories.longDescription: PineSemi's intelligent power modules (IPMs) combine power semiconductors with integrated gate drivers and protection circuits in compact packages. These ready-to-use modules simplify inverter design by eliminating the need for external gate drive circuits and complex protection implementations. The IPM portfolio covers power ratings from 100W to 5kW, serving applications such as home appliances, HVAC systems, servo drives, and industrial automation. Advanced features include built-in protection against overcurrent, short-circuit, undervoltage, and overtemperature; fault feedback signals for system monitoring; and optimized switching characteristics for low EMI. The highly integrated design reduces BOM count, PCB size, and development time while improving system reliability.

series.name: IPM-S Series
series.description: Standard IPMs for general-purpose motor control

series.name: IPM-A Series
series.description: Advanced IPMs with enhanced protection and diagnostics

selectionGuide.title: How to Select PineSemi Intelligent Power Modules
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting IPMs based on power level, voltage, and application requirements

faqs.question: What are the advantages of using IPMs vs discrete solutions?
faqs.answer: IPMs offer significant advantages over discrete designs: Integration - gate drivers, protection, and power devices in one package; Reduced design time - no need to design gate drive and protection circuits; Smaller PCB size - highly integrated package reduces footprint; Improved reliability - factory-tested and optimized matching; Lower BOM cost - fewer components overall; Better performance - optimized switching characteristics. Trade-offs: Less flexibility - fixed configurations; Higher module cost vs discrete components; Limited scalability - power rating is fixed. IPMs are ideal for: Time-to-market critical projects; Cost-sensitive consumer applications; Designs where reliability is paramount; Engineers without deep power electronics expertise. For high-volume or specialized applications, discrete designs may offer better optimization.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for guidance on IPM vs discrete solutions for your specific application.

faqs.question: What protection features are included in PineSemi IPMs?
faqs.answer: PineSemi IPMs include comprehensive protection features: Overcurrent protection - internal current sensing with fast shutdown (< 2μs); Short-circuit protection - desaturation detection for IGBTs; Undervoltage lockout - prevents operation with insufficient gate voltage; Overtemperature protection - internal temperature sensor with programmable threshold; Fault reporting - dedicated fault output pin indicates protection activation; Soft shutdown - controlled turn-off during faults to prevent voltage spikes. Protection behavior: Automatic - module shuts down when fault detected; Latched - fault remains until reset signal applied; Reported - fault pin indicates type of fault (some models). These integrated protections eliminate the need for external protection circuits and provide reliable fault handling.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for detailed protection specifications and fault handling guidelines.

faqs.question: How do I interface with PineSemi IPMs?
faqs.answer: PineSemi IPM interface connections: Power connections - DC bus input, motor phase outputs; Control interface - logic-level inputs (3.3V/5V compatible); Control signals - U, V, W phase inputs (typically active high); Enable - master enable/disable input; Fault output - open-drain fault indication; Reset - fault clear input (on advanced models). Control characteristics: Input logic - compatible with 3.3V and 5V microcontrollers; Input filtering - built-in noise filtering (typically 1-2μs); Dead time - internal dead time insertion prevents shoot-through; Isolation - control inputs are isolated from power section (2.5kV). Interface is straightforward - connect directly to microcontroller PWM outputs. No external gate drivers or level shifters needed. Fault output can be connected to microcontroller interrupt or fault input.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for interface design guidelines and microcontroller integration examples.

faqs.question: What is the maximum switching frequency for PineSemi IPMs?
faqs.answer: Maximum switching frequencies for PineSemi IPMs: Standard IPMs (IPM-S series): 20kHz maximum recommended; Advanced IPMs (IPM-A series): 30kHz maximum recommended; Practical operating frequencies: 4-16kHz for motor drives; 20kHz for low-noise applications. Limitations: Internal gate drive capability - higher frequencies require more gate drive power; Thermal considerations - switching losses increase with frequency; Dead time - minimum dead time limits maximum frequency. For higher frequencies (>20kHz), consider: Using advanced IPM series with better gate drivers; Reducing switching speed (external gate resistor); Improving thermal management; Using discrete SiC solutions for very high frequencies. Most motor drive applications operate well within 20kHz limit.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for switching frequency recommendations based on your application requirements.

faqs.question: How do I size an IPM for my motor drive application?
faqs.answer: IPM sizing procedure for motor drives: Motor current - determine RMS motor current at maximum load; Overload capability - consider motor overload requirements (typically 150% for 1 minute); Switching frequency - higher frequencies reduce usable current due to switching losses; Thermal design - ensure adequate heatsinking for continuous operation. Sizing formula: IPM current rating > Motor RMS current x 1.2 (safety margin); Consider overload: IPM overload capability > Motor overload requirement. Example: 5HP (3.7kW) motor, 9A RMS current, 150% overload: Select IPM rated for > 11A continuous with > 14A overload capability. Voltage rating: Match to DC bus voltage with 20% margin; 600V IPM for 380-460V AC input (rectified to ~650V DC); 1200V IPM for higher voltage applications. PineSemi provides selection guides for common motor sizes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for IPM selection assistance and thermal design support for your motor drive.

products.name: 15A 600V Standard IPM
products.shortDescription: 15A 600V intelligent power module with integrated gate drivers and protection for motor drives up to 1.5kW.
descriptionParagraphs: The IPM-S15A-6 is a 15A 600V intelligent power module designed for general-purpose motor control applications.
Integrates six IGBTs with gate drivers, protection circuits, and fault feedback in a compact package.
Ideal for home appliances, HVAC systems, and small industrial drives requiring reliable motor control.
features: Six IGBTs with integrated gate drivers

faeReview.author: Liu Yang
faeReview.title: FAE - Appliance Motor Control
faeReview.content: The IPM-S15A-6 is a workhorse for appliance motor control applications. I've used this IPM in numerous washing machine and air conditioner designs, and it consistently delivers reliable performance. The integration of gate drivers and protection circuits significantly reduces design time - what used to take weeks of gate drive design now takes hours. The protection features are robust - overcurrent detection is fast and reliable. In a recent washing machine project, this IPM handled the high-torque startup and variable speed operation flawlessly. The 3.3V logic compatibility makes it easy to interface with modern microcontrollers. For appliance applications where cost and reliability are critical, this IPM offers excellent value.
faeReview.highlight: Reliable and cost-effective solution for appliance motor control

alternativeParts.reason: Lower current option for smaller motors
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for small fans, pumps, or compressors under 1kW

alternativeParts.reason: Higher current option for larger motors
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for larger HVAC systems or industrial pumps

companionParts.description: Motor control microcontroller with FOC library
DC-link film capacitor for IPM applications

faqs.question: How do I connect control signals to IPM-S15A-6?
faqs.answer: Control signal connections for IPM-S15A-6: Power supply - provide isolated 15V supply for gate drive (typically 50-100mA); Control inputs - UH, UL, VH, VL, WH, WL (high and low side for each phase); Logic level - compatible with 3.3V and 5V CMOS outputs; Input current - < 1mA per input (high impedance); Enable - active high enable input (pull low to disable); Fault output - open-drain, requires external pull-up resistor; Reset - pulse low to clear fault (if latched). Connection diagram: Connect microcontroller PWM outputs directly to UH, UL, VH, VL, WH, WL; Connect enable to microcontroller GPIO; Connect fault to microcontroller interrupt input with pull-up; Provide external reset signal if needed. No external gate resistors or drivers required. Built-in dead time prevents shoot-through.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for connection diagrams and microcontroller interface examples.

faqs.question: What is the fault behavior of IPM-S15A-6?
faqs.answer: Fault operation of IPM-S15A-6: Fault types detected: Overcurrent - fast shutdown when current exceeds threshold; Short-circuit - desaturation detection during conduction; Undervoltage - shutdown when Vcc drops below UVLO threshold; Overtemperature - shutdown when internal temperature exceeds limit. Fault response: Automatic shutdown of all IGBTs within 2μs; Fault pin pulled low to indicate fault condition; Latched state - remains shutdown until reset; Reset - apply low pulse to reset pin or cycle power. Fault handling in software: Monitor fault pin with interrupt; When fault detected, stop PWM generation; Implement fault logging and diagnostics; Apply reset after fault condition cleared; Implement retry logic with backoff if needed. The integrated protection eliminates need for external protection circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for fault handling guidelines and software implementation examples.

faqs.question: How much heatsinking does IPM-S15A-6 require?
faqs.answer: Thermal design for IPM-S15A-6: Power losses at 15A, 8kHz, PF=0.8: approximately 25-30W total; Thermal resistance junction-to-case: 2.5 K/W per IGBT; Maximum junction temperature: 150°C; Recommended operating: < 125°C for reliability. Heatsink calculation: Target case temperature: < 100°C at worst case; Required Rth(s-a): (100°C - Ta) / Ploss; Example: Ta=50°C, Ploss=30W: Rth(s-a) < (100-50)/30 = 1.67 K/W. Heatsink options: Natural convection: Large heatsink with fins; Forced air: Smaller heatsink with fan; Cold plate: Liquid cooling for compact designs. Thermal interface: Use thermal pad or grease between module and heatsink; Mounting torque: 0.8-1.2 Nm per screw. Proper thermal design is critical for reliable operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for thermal design support and heatsink recommendations.

faqs.question: Can IPM-S15A-6 be used with different PWM modulation schemes?
faqs.answer: Yes, IPM-S15A-6 supports various PWM modulation schemes: Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) - standard voltage-source inverter modulation; Space vector PWM (SVPWM) - more efficient use of DC bus voltage; Third harmonic injection - increases voltage utilization by 15%; Discontinuous PWM - reduces switching losses at high modulation index. Implementation: IPM responds to logic-level PWM inputs; Dead time is internally generated (typically 1.5-2.5μs); Minimum pulse width: typically 1μs (check datasheet); Maximum duty cycle: limited by dead time requirements. The IPM is modulation-agnostic - it simply switches based on input signals. Advanced motor control algorithms (FOC, DTC) can be implemented in microcontroller. The IPM handles the power switching while microcontroller handles control algorithm.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for PWM modulation guidelines and motor control algorithm support.

faqs.question: What microcontroller is recommended for IPM-S15A-6?
faqs.answer: Recommended microcontrollers for IPM-S15A-6: Requirements: 6 PWM outputs (3-phase with complementary signals); 3.3V or 5V logic levels; Motor control features (encoder inputs, ADC for current sensing); Processing power for control algorithm (FOC, V/Hz, etc.). Recommended families: STM32 (STM32F0, F3, G0, G4 series) - excellent motor control features; Microchip dsPIC - dedicated motor control DSCs; TI C2000 - optimized for power conversion; NXP LPC or Kinetis - good motor control support. For simple V/Hz control: Basic 8-bit or 32-bit MCU sufficient; For FOC (sensorless or sensored): 32-bit MCU with FPU recommended (72MHz+). PineSemi provides example code and reference designs for popular microcontroller families. The IPM's simple interface works with any microcontroller that can generate PWM signals.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for microcontroller recommendations and reference code for your motor control application.

categories.name: SiC Diodes
categories.description: PineSemi's silicon carbide Schottky diodes offer zero reverse recovery and high-temperature operation for efficient power conversion.
categories.longDescription: PineSemi's silicon carbide (SiC) Schottky diode portfolio delivers breakthrough performance for power rectification applications. Unlike silicon diodes, SiC Schottky diodes exhibit zero reverse recovery charge and extremely fast switching, virtually eliminating switching losses associated with diode recovery. Available in voltage ratings from 650V to 1200V and current ratings from 5A to 60A, these diodes are ideal for power factor correction, solar inverters, motor drives, and high-frequency power supplies. The high-temperature capability (up to 175°C junction temperature) and low leakage current make them suitable for demanding automotive and industrial applications. When paired with IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs, PineSemi SiC diodes enable significant efficiency improvements and reduced EMI.

series.name: PSD1 Series
series.description: 650V SiC Schottky diodes for PFC and DC-DC applications

series.name: PSD2 Series
series.description: 1200V SiC Schottky diodes for high-voltage applications

selectionGuide.title: How to Select PineSemi SiC Diodes
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting SiC Schottky diodes based on voltage, current, and application requirements

faqs.question: What are the advantages of SiC Schottky diodes over silicon diodes?
faqs.answer: SiC Schottky diodes offer major advantages: Zero reverse recovery - eliminates switching losses from diode recovery; Fast switching - no reverse recovery time enables higher frequencies; Temperature stability - characteristics remain stable across temperature range; High temperature operation - up to 175°C vs 150°C for silicon; Low EMI - soft switching reduces electromagnetic interference. Comparison with silicon: Silicon PN diodes have significant Qrr (10-100μC) causing switching losses; Silicon Schottky diodes have high leakage at high temperatures; SiC diodes maintain low leakage and stable Vf at high temperatures. Applications benefiting most: PFC circuits, bridgeless topologies, high-frequency converters, motor drives with hard switching. The efficiency improvement often justifies higher cost.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for detailed comparison and ROI analysis for your application.

faqs.question: When should I use SiC diodes with IGBTs?
faqs.answer: Use SiC diodes with IGBTs when: High-frequency operation - above 20kHz where diode recovery losses become significant; Bridgeless PFC - continuous diode conduction makes recovery critical; Hard-switching inverters - recovery losses affect IGBT turn-on; Efficiency is critical - SiC diodes improve system efficiency by 0.5-1%; EMI reduction - soft switching reduces noise and filtering requirements. Cost-benefit analysis: SiC diodes cost 3-5x more than silicon fast recovery diodes; Break-even depends on operating frequency and power level; Typically justified for: >5kW systems, >20kHz operation, or efficiency-critical applications. Not necessary when: Low frequency operation (<10kHz); Soft-switching topologies; Cost is primary concern; Silicon fast recovery diodes sufficient. Many designs use SiC diodes only for critical positions (e.g., boost diode in PFC) and silicon elsewhere.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for cost-benefit analysis and application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I select the right SiC diode current rating?
faqs.answer: SiC diode current rating selection: Average forward current - select based on RMS current in application; Surge current - ensure adequate non-repetitive surge capability; Thermal design - consider Rth and cooling for continuous operation. Selection guidelines: PFC applications: Diode current = 1.5-2x output current (due to duty cycle); Inverter applications: Match to IGBT/switch current rating; Rectifier applications: Based on load current and conduction angle. Example - 10kW PFC at 400V output: Output current = 25A DC; Diode average current at 50% duty cycle = 25A; Select 30-40A rated diode for margin. Temperature derating: Current rating decreases at high temperatures; Use derating curves from datasheet; Design for worst-case operating temperature. PineSemi provides selection guides for common applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for diode sizing assistance and thermal design support.

faqs.question: What is the forward voltage drop of PineSemi SiC diodes?
faqs.answer: Forward voltage characteristics of PineSemi SiC diodes: Typical Vf at rated current: 1.3-1.5V (higher than silicon Schottky, lower than silicon PN); Temperature coefficient: +0.1 to +0.2 mV/°C (positive, unlike silicon); Low current Vf: Higher than silicon at light loads; High current Vf: More stable than silicon across temperature. Comparison at 25°C, 20A: Silicon fast recovery: 1.0-1.2V; Silicon Schottky: 0.7-0.9V; SiC Schottky: 1.3-1.5V. At 125°C: Silicon fast recovery: 0.9-1.0V; Silicon Schottky: 0.5-0.6V (but high leakage); SiC Schottky: 1.4-1.6V (stable). While SiC Vf is higher, the zero recovery eliminates switching losses, resulting in net efficiency improvement in high-frequency applications. Conduction losses are offset by elimination of recovery losses.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for loss calculations comparing SiC and silicon diodes for your application.

faqs.question: Can SiC diodes be paralleled for higher current?
faqs.answer: Yes, SiC Schottky diodes can be paralleled for higher current: Current sharing - positive temperature coefficient of Vf promotes natural current sharing; Matching - use diodes from same production lot for best matching; Layout - symmetrical layout with equal trace lengths; Thermal - ensure equal thermal resistance to heatsink. Paralleling guidelines: 2-4 diodes in parallel is typical; Current sharing typically within 10% with good layout; No current-sharing resistors needed (unlike silicon diodes); Individual fusing recommended for protection. Design considerations: Voltage derating - parallel diodes don't increase voltage rating; Thermal design - calculate losses for total current; Switching - paralleling doesn't affect switching characteristics. For very high currents, consider using larger single diodes or module packages instead of many parallel discrete devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for paralleling guidelines and layout recommendations.

products.name: 650V 60A SiC Schottky Diode
products.shortDescription: 650V 60A silicon carbide Schottky diode with zero reverse recovery for high-efficiency PFC and rectification applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The PSD1-65H060 is a 650V 60A SiC Schottky diode featuring zero reverse recovery charge and fast switching characteristics.
Designed for high-efficiency power factor correction, boost converters, and high-frequency rectification.
The TO-247 package provides excellent thermal performance and compatibility with standard heatsinks.
features: Fast switching with no recovery time

faeReview.author: Zhou Tao
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Power Conversion
faeReview.content: The PSD1-65H060 is my go-to recommendation for high-power PFC applications. I've specified this diode in numerous 10-20kW server power supplies and the performance is consistently excellent. The zero reverse recovery eliminates the switching losses that plague silicon fast recovery diodes at high frequencies. In a recent 15kW titanium-efficiency PSU design, replacing silicon diodes with these SiC devices improved PFC efficiency by 0.8% and eliminated the need for snubber circuits. The 60A rating provides good margin for 30-40A PFC stages, and the TO-247 package makes thermal management straightforward. For high-frequency PFC where efficiency matters, this diode pays for itself in reduced cooling costs.
faeReview.highlight: Excellent choice for high-power PFC with proven efficiency gains

alternativeParts.reason: Lower current option for smaller PFC stages
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for PFC stages up to 5kW
Use for high-voltage PFC or EV onboard chargers

faqs.question: How much efficiency improvement can PSD1-65H060 provide in PFC?
faqs.answer: Efficiency improvements with PSD1-65H060 in PFC: Typical improvement: 0.5-1.0% over silicon fast recovery diodes; Higher at high frequencies: up to 1.5% at 100kHz; Depends on operating conditions: Input voltage, output power, switching frequency. Loss comparison at 10kW, 400V output, 50kHz: Silicon fast recovery: 15-20W diode losses (conduction + recovery); SiC Schottky: 8-10W losses (conduction only). The efficiency improvement is most significant in: Bridgeless totem-pole PFC (diode conducts continuously); High-frequency operation (>50kHz); High-voltage input applications (277V AC); Systems targeting 80Plus Titanium or better. Additional benefits: Reduced heatsink requirements; Lower EMI (no recovery spikes); Elimination of snubber circuits; Improved reliability (lower temperature).
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for efficiency calculations and ROI analysis for your PFC design.

faqs.question: What is the surge current capability of PSD1-65H060?
faqs.answer: Surge current specifications for PSD1-65H060: Non-repetitive surge current: 300A for 10ms (half-sine wave); Repetitive surge current: 100A peak; Surge capability is important for: Inrush current during startup; Line surge events; Capacitor charging current. The SiC Schottky structure provides good surge capability, though generally lower than silicon PN diodes. For applications with high inrush current: Consider using inrush current limiters (NTC thermistors); Implement soft-start circuits; Size diode with surge margin. The TO-247 package provides good thermal mass to handle short-duration surges. For repetitive surge conditions, ensure average current rating is not exceeded.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for surge current analysis and protection design recommendations.

faqs.question: How does forward voltage vary with temperature for PSD1-65H060?
faqs.answer: Temperature characteristics of PSD1-65H060 forward voltage: At 25°C: 1.45V typical @ 60A; At 100°C: 1.55V typical @ 60A; At 150°C: 1.65V typical @ 60A; Temperature coefficient: +0.15 mV/°C (positive). This positive temperature coefficient is beneficial: Natural current sharing when paralleling diodes; Reduced risk of thermal runaway; Stable operation across temperature range. Comparison with silicon: Silicon PN diodes have negative temperature coefficient (-2 mV/°C); Silicon Schottky have strong negative coefficient; SiC Schottky have slight positive coefficient. For thermal design: Use Vf at maximum operating temperature; Calculate conduction losses at worst-case temperature; The increase in Vf is offset by elimination of recovery losses at high temperatures.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for thermal design support and loss calculations across temperature range.

faqs.question: Can PSD1-65H060 be used as a freewheeling diode?
faqs.answer: Yes, PSD1-65H060 can be used as a freewheeling diode: Advantages: Zero reverse recovery eliminates turn-on losses in switch; Fast switching reduces dead time requirements; Low switching losses enable higher frequencies. Considerations: Forward voltage (1.45V) is higher than silicon Schottky (0.8V); Conduction losses may be higher at low frequencies; Cost is higher than silicon alternatives. Best applications: High-frequency converters (>50kHz); Synchronous rectification replacement; Bridge configurations with hard switching; EV onboard chargers and DC-DC. For low-frequency motor drives (<10kHz), silicon fast recovery diodes may be more cost-effective. The decision depends on operating frequency, efficiency requirements, and cost targets.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for freewheeling diode selection based on your switching frequency and efficiency requirements.

faqs.question: What is the reverse leakage current of PSD1-65H060?
faqs.answer: Reverse leakage characteristics of PSD1-65H060: At 25°C, 650V: < 100μA typical; At 100°C, 650V: < 500μA typical; At 150°C, 650V: < 2mA typical. Leakage current increases with temperature but remains low compared to silicon Schottky diodes. Impact on efficiency: At 650V, 100μA = 0.065W (negligible); At 150°C, 2mA = 1.3W (still small compared to conduction losses). Comparison with silicon: Silicon Schottky: 10-100x higher leakage at high temperatures; Silicon PN: Lower leakage but has recovery losses. The low leakage of SiC diodes is a significant advantage at high temperatures. For high-voltage applications, leakage power remains small compared to switching losses saved.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us for leakage current specifications and high-temperature operation guidelines.
